name: Three-Phase Sweep
summary: Copy intervals that end before the new one, absorb every interval that overlaps it into a growing merged interval, place it, then copy the rest.
visualizationIntent: init: the sorted intervals appear alongside an empty result and the new interval's start/end. loop 1: pointer i copies intervals that end before the new interval starts into result. loop 2: intervals whose start is within the new interval's end are absorbed, growing new_start and new_end. update: the merged new interval is appended into result. loop 3: pointer i copies the remaining intervals. return: result is the sorted, non-overlapping list with the new interval merged in.

lineExplanations.3: Create an empty result list to build the answer in order.
lineExplanations.4: Take the start of the interval we are inserting.
lineExplanations.5: Take the end of the interval we are inserting.
lineExplanations.6: Start scanning the existing intervals from the first one.
lineExplanations.7: Remember how many existing intervals there are.
lineExplanations.8: Phase 1: while an interval ends before the new interval starts, it stays untouched.
lineExplanations.9: Copy that entirely-earlier interval straight into the result.
lineExplanations.11: Phase 2: while an interval starts on or before the new interval's end, it overlaps.
lineExplanations.12: Absorb it: pull the merged start left to the smaller of the two starts.
lineExplanations.13: Absorb it: push the merged end right to the larger of the two ends.
lineExplanations.15: Place the fully merged new interval into the result.
lineExplanations.16: Phase 3: copy every remaining interval, which all sit after the new one.
lineExplanations.17: Copy the later interval into the result.
lineExplanations.19: Return the intervals with the new one inserted and merged.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: defines the class wrapper LeetCode expects.
syntaxExplanations.2: def insert(self, intervals, newInterval): receives the sorted intervals and the interval to insert.
syntaxExplanations.4: newInterval[0] reads the new interval's start.
syntaxExplanations.5: newInterval[1] reads the new interval's end.
syntaxExplanations.6: i = 0 starts the scan index at the first interval.
syntaxExplanations.7: n = len(intervals) counts the existing intervals.
syntaxExplanations.8: while i < n and intervals[i][1] < new_start: 'and' short-circuits so intervals[i] is only read when i < n.
syntaxExplanations.10: i += 1 moves to the next interval.
syntaxExplanations.11: while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= new_end: keeps going while intervals overlap the new one.
syntaxExplanations.12: min(new_start, intervals[i][0]) keeps the smaller start.
syntaxExplanations.13: max(new_end, intervals[i][1]) keeps the larger end.
syntaxExplanations.14: i += 1 moves past the absorbed interval.
syntaxExplanations.15: result.append([new_start, new_end]) adds the merged new interval.
syntaxExplanations.16: while i < n: copies the remaining intervals.
syntaxExplanations.18: i += 1 moves to the next interval.
syntaxExplanations.19: return result hands back the final list.
